message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate febrile seizure risk following monovalent COVID-19 mRNA vaccination among children aged 2-5 years.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The primary analysis evaluated children who had a febrile seizure outcome in the 0-1 days following COVID-19 vaccination. A self-controlled case series analysis was performed in three commercial insurance databases to compare the risk of seizure in the risk interval (0-1 days) to a control interval (8-63 days).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The exposure of interest was receipt of dose 1 and/or dose 2 of monovalent COVID-19 mRNA vaccinations.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The primary outcome was febrile seizure (0-1 day risk interval).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>A conditional Poisson regression model was used to compare outcome rates in risk and control intervals and estimate incidence rate ratios (IRR) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Meta-analyses were used to pool results across databases.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The primary meta-analysis found a statistically significant increased incidence of febrile seizure, in the 0-1 days following mRNA-1273 vaccination compared to the control interval (IRR: 2.52, 95% CI: 1.35 to 4.69, risk difference (RD)/100,000 doses = 3.22 (95%CI -0.31 to 6.75)). For the BNT162b2 vaccination, the IRR was elevated but not statistically significant (IRR: 1.41, 95%CI: 0.48 to 4.11, RD/100,000 doses = -0.25 (95%CI -2.75 to 2.24).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Among children aged 2-5 years, the analysis showed a small elevated incidence rate ratio of febrile seizures in the 0-1 days following the mRNA-1273 vaccination. Based on the current body of scientific evidence, the safety profile of the monovalent mRNA vaccines remains favorable for use in young children.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Article Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this self-controlled case series study, researchers evaluated whether there is an elevated risk of febrile seizure immediately following monovalent COVID-19 mRNA vaccination.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What’s Known on This Subject</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The United States Food and Drug Administration previously noted a potential safety concern for seizure in children aged 2-5 years receiving the ancestral monovalent COVID-19 mRNA vaccines.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What This Study Adds</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this self-controlled case series that included participants aged 2-5 years from three commercial insurance databases, the incidence rate ratio of febrile seizures was significantly elevated in the 0-1 days following mRNA-1273 administration. Absolute risk was small.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Febrile Seizure Risk Following Monovalent COVID-19 mRNA Vaccination in US Children Aged 2-5 Years
